meta.description: The Stoic theory of value: genuine goods, genuine evils, and indifferents with varying degrees of selective value. V1 called this 'indifferents.json' and used 3 flat categories. The sources describe a richer hierarchy: genuine goods (only virtue), genuine evils (only vice), and a continuum of selective value among indifferents.
meta.derivation: Stobaeus Eclogae Section 3 ('On the Classification of Goods and Ills') treats value classification as a standalone major section, including the 3-location model of goods. DL Lives 7.101-107 provides the formal indifferents taxonomy. Stobaeus 2.79-85 preserves Chrysippus on selective value (axia). Cicero De Finibus 3.50-57 covers preferred/dispreferred. Seneca De Vita Beata 22-24 treats wealth as material for virtue.
primary_sources: Stobaeus, Eclogae Section 3 — classification of goods and ills, 3-location model
Stobaeus, Eclogae 2.79-85 — Chrysippus on selective value (axia)
Cicero, De Finibus 3.50-57 — preferred and dispreferred indifferents
Seneca, De Vita Beata 22-24 — wealth as material for virtue

hierarchy.description: The fundamental Stoic value hierarchy. Only virtue is genuinely good. Only vice is genuinely evil. Everything else is indifferent — but indifferents possess varying degrees of selective value (axia).

genuine_goods.description: Only virtue (arete) and what participates in virtue. These alone make the possessor better and constitute the basis for eudaimonia.

stobaeus_three_locations.description: Stobaeus Section 3 classifies goods by three locations. This is the Stoic taxonomy of goods.
stobaeus_three_locations.source: Stobaeus Ecl. Section 3: 'Some goods are about the soul, some external, some neither about the soul nor external.'

locations.name: Goods of the Soul
locations.description: The virtues themselves and the right activities that flow from them. The primary goods.
locations.stobaeus: 'About the soul are the virtues and the worthy pursuits and in general the praiseworthy activities.'

locations.name: External Goods
locations.description: Things outside the soul that participate in virtue.
locations.stobaeus: 'External are friends and acquaintances and similar things.'
locations.note: These are genuinely good because they participate in virtue, not because they are pleasant or useful.

locations.name: Neither Soul Nor External
locations.description: The virtuous person themselves — considered as a whole.
locations.stobaeus: 'Neither about the soul nor external are the worthy pursuers and in general those possessing the virtues.'

all_goods_are_beneficial.stobaeus: 'All goods are beneficial and useful and advantageous and profitable and worthy of serious pursuit and becoming and beautiful and familiar.'

multiple_meanings_of_good.stobaeus: 'They say "good" is spoken in multiple ways: the primary, as it were occupying the place of a source — which is thus defined: that from which it happens to be benefited, or by which benefit occurs.'

genuine_evils.description: Only vice (kakia) and what participates in vice. These alone make the possessor worse.
genuine_evils.stobaeus_parallel: Stobaeus mirrors the goods structure: 'Of ills, some are about the soul, some external, some neither. About the soul are the vices with the depraved dispositions and in general the blameworthy activities.'

indifferents.description: Everything that is neither genuinely good nor genuinely evil. These possess selective value (axia) but not genuine goodness (agathon).

selective_value.description: The Stoic concept of axia — selective value. NOT the same as genuine goodness. It is the degree to which something naturally promotes or hinders the functioning of a rational being.
selective_value.source: Stobaeus Ecl. 2.83: 'preferred indifferents have selective value (eklektike axia) but not genuine goodness'
selective_value.continuum: Selective value operates on a continuum from strongly preferred to strongly dispreferred, with neutral at zero. V1's three flat categories (preferred/dispreferred/neutral) are simplified labels for regions of this continuum.

preferred.label: Preferred (proegmenon)
preferred.description: According to nature (kata physin). Possesses positive selective value. The sage will select these over their opposites, all else being equal.

items.name: Life
items.note: Foundational — the condition for all virtuous activity

items.name: Wealth
items.note: Material for just action (Seneca De Vita Beata 22-24)
items.source: DL 7.102; Stobaeus Ecl. 2.80; Seneca De Vita Beata 22-24

dispreferred.label: Dispreferred (apoproegmenon)
dispreferred.description: Against nature (para physin). Possesses negative selective value. The sage will avoid these when possible.

items.name: Poverty
items.source: DL 7.102; Stobaeus Ecl. 2.80; Seneca De Vita Beata 22

neutral.label: Neutral (meson)
neutral.description: Zero selective value — genuinely neither preferred nor dispreferred.
examples: Whether one has an odd or even number of hairs (DL Lives 7.104)
Whether one extends or contracts a finger

selection_principles.description: How to choose among indifferents. These are source-grounded principles, not SageReasoning design.
selection_principles.source: Stobaeus Eclogae 2.83-84; Cicero De Finibus 3.50-57; Cicero De Officiis 3.7-19

rules.rule: Select what is kata physin (according to nature) over what is para physin (against nature)
Among preferred indifferents, select based on degree of selective value (axia)
Select what serves one's kathekonta (appropriate actions) in current roles
rules.source: Stobaeus: the preferred indifferents are 'selected because they are contrary to nature and according to nature, offering such a state of affairs that unless we obtain them or are deprived of them without complication, we would not flourish.'

rules.rule: NEVER select an indifferent at the cost of virtue — the honourable always prevails
rules.source: Cicero De Officiis 3.7-19: 'Nothing that is morally base can be truly advantageous'

rules.rule: The sage selects (eklegetai) preferred indifferents — they do not desire (oregetai) them. Selection is rational; desire is passion.
